column1: Inhalational agents — 3 factors determining speed of alveolar delivery?
column2:
• Inspired concentration of gas
  • Gas flow rate through the machine
  • Alveolar ventilation (respiratory rate × tidal volume)

column1: Inhalational agents — 3 factors determining speed of alveolar-brain equilibration?
column2:
• Rate of blood flow to the brain
  • Solubility of the agent (blood:gas coefficient)
  • Arteriovenous concentration difference of the agent

column1: Low cardiac output — explain the vicious cycle with volatile anaesthetics.
column2:
↓ CO → ↓ alveolar drainage → ↑ FA → ↑ arterial partial pressure → deeper anaesthesia → ↑ cardiovascular depression (negative inotropy + vasodilation) → ↓ CO further → ↑ FA → deeper still.

Reduce vaporiser setting proactively in shocked or low-CO patients.

column1: Second gas effect — mechanism and clinical relevance?
column2:
N2O is 34× more soluble in blood than nitrogen.
On induction: N2O rapidly leaves the alveolus into blood → N2 cannot leave tissues quickly enough to fill the space → residual alveolar gases (O2 and co-administered volatiles) become concentrated → ↑ FA of co-agent → faster onset of inhalational effect.

On N2O discontinuation: body stores of N2O flood back into alveoli → displaces alveolar O2 → risk of hypoxaemia.

Prevention: Administer 100% O2 at end of anaesthesia.

• Pneumothorax
  • Closed loop bowel obstruction
  • Air embolism
  • Intracranial air
  • Middle ear / sinus surgery (relative)
  • Vitreoretinal gas tamponade
All involve closed air space expansion.
